facts.fact: TLS 1.2 fresh connection requires 3 total RTTs: 1 for TCP three-way handshake + 2 for TLS negotiation, translating to 240ms of pure handshake overhead on an 80ms RTT mobile network
TLS 1.3 reduces handshake from 2 RTTs to 1 RTT by having client send key share in first message, saving ~80-100ms on typical connections
Production deployment found TLS 1.2 required ~40M full handshakes while TLS 1.3 required ~6M, resulting in $25,200 annual AWS ALB cost savings
Session resumption costs less than 50% of a full TLS handshake because it requires only 1 RTT while full handshake requires 2 RTTs
Session tickets eliminate server-side session storage burden, allowing stateless session resumption - client stores ticket and keying material
Full TLS handshakes consume 5-10x more CPU than resumed sessions due to expensive asymmetric cryptography operations
Production sites target >80% TLS session resumption rates; if resumption drops below 50%, CPU costs spike dramatically
TLS 1.3 0-RTT mode allows client to include encrypted application data in first packet for repeat connections, eliminating handshake latency
SNI (Server Name Indication) is sent unencrypted in ClientHello, making hostname visible to passive eavesdroppers; Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) in RFC 9849 (March 2026) encrypts full ClientHello
AWS ALB automatically selects optimal TLS certificate for each client when multiple certificates bound to same listener via SNI
Layer 4 load balancers route on IP/port without decryption, enabling fast secure routing; Layer 7 requires TLS termination and re-encryption to backends
Layer 4 + SNI routing allows TCP load balancers to inspect SNI/ALPN in TLS handshake and forward connection to correct backend without decryption
OCSP stapling eliminates 100-300ms latency from client querying CA separately for revocation status by attaching signed OCSP response to handshake
OCSP stapling can accelerate TLS handshakes by up to 30% in high-volume networks
Static content should use Cache-Control with max-age >6 months, public directive, and immutable flag for cache-busted versioned resources
Dynamic content requires no-cache directive for HTML to force revalidation; low TTL + private cache for personalized data
ETag provides unique validation token for cache revalidation; Last-Modified provides timestamp; both prevent unnecessary content re-fetch
TTFB benchmarks: <100ms fantastic, 200-500ms standard, 500ms-1s less than ideal, >1s requires investigation
Good TTFB values are ≤0.8 seconds; poor values >1.8 seconds; most sites should target <0.8s
Primary cause of high TTFB is dynamic content generation (PHP, database queries) not latency; slow queries delay responses even with CDN
Chrome DevTools Network tab shows TTFB breakdown in waterfall: queuing + stalled + DNS lookup + TCP connection + TLS handshake + request time + server processing
Certificate chain length adds latency; longer chains require more certificate verification and intermediary CA lookups
Certificate pinning requires clients to maintain trusted certificate/key list, but continuous updates for renewals and rotations create operational burden
Industry consensus: certificate pinning creates more risk than reward; Certificate Transparency logs provide better detection without pinning burden
QUIC + HTTP/3 establishes fresh connection in 1-RTT and 0-RTT for repeat connections with application data included
HTTP/3 saves 50% connection time vs HTTP/2 with TLS 1.2 by combining TLS handshake with HTTP request
Google measured QUIC reducing Search latency by 3.6% and YouTube video buffering by 15.3%
QUIC streams are independent; packet loss on one stream doesn't affect others, unlike TCP head-of-line blocking
TCP three-way handshake introduces 1 RTT latency before data transmission; for short flows with high network latency (100s of ms), multiple RTTs cause visible user impact
Fresh HTTPS connection over TLS 1.2: DNS (1 RTT) + TCP (1 RTT) + TLS (2 RTT) = 4 RTT minimum before first byte on intercontinental links

examples.example: Netflix observes that 250ms additional latency results in measurable revenue impact across streaming signup flows
examples.context: Demonstrates real business impact of HTTPS handshake latency

examples.example: AWS ALB deployed at scale with multiple domains (e.g., api.example.com, cdn.example.com) uses SNI to serve different certificates from single IP:port without Layer 7 termination overhead
Cloudflare enables OCSP stapling by default across all proxied domains, reducing client-CA round-trips for millions of daily HTTPS connections
examples.context: Large-scale deployment of revocation checking optimization

examples.example: WordPress sites using caching plugins (e.g., WP Super Cache) can reduce TTFB by 200-400ms by converting dynamic pages to static HTML
examples.context: Common TTFB optimization for CMS platforms

examples.example: Google Search and YouTube measured 3.6% latency reduction and 15.3% faster video buffering after enabling QUIC/HTTP3

analogies.analogy: TLS 1.2 handshake is like a letter-writing negotiation: client sends proposal (1 RTT), server responds with terms (1 RTT), client confirms agreement. TLS 1.3 is like client arriving with proposal already signed, skipping the counter-proposal round.

Session resumption is like showing a membership card instead of filling out a full application. Session tickets are like laminated membership cards clients carry (no server storage); session IDs are like server keeping a giant rolodex of every member.
analogies.context: Explains session resumption mechanisms and scalability tradeoffs

analogies.analogy: SNI is like addressing an envelope with the recipient's name so the mail carrier (load balancer) can route it to the right household (backend service) before opening it.
analogies.context: Explains how SNI enables Layer 4 TLS routing

analogies.analogy: OCSP stapling is like including a recent government ID photo in a letter instead of the recipient having to call the government to verify your identity.
analogies.context: Explains how OCSP stapling eliminates CA round-trips

analogies.analogy: Certificate pinning is like supergluing a specific key to your door frame to prevent master keys from opening it. Works perfectly until you need to change locks (certificate renewal).
analogies.context: Explains the rigidity and operational burden of pinning

analogies.analogy: QUIC combining TLS + connection setup is like synchronized swimming: TCP and TLS do separate choreography (multiple steps), while QUIC combines them into one coordinated routine.
analogies.context: Explains why HTTP/3 reduces connection latency

analogies.analogy: TTFB waterfall is like tracking a package: queuing at post office → shipping → delivery truck arrival → door knock → recipient opening door (server processing) → first item visible. Slow server processing is the final step, but often blamed on earlier steps.
analogies.context: Explains why TTFB diagnosis requires waterfall analysis, not just total time

common_misconceptions.misconception: TTFB is primarily a network latency problem
common_misconceptions.reality: TTFB = network latency + server processing time. Majority of high TTFB is server-side (slow database queries, rendering), not network RTT. CDNs don't help if server is slow.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TLS handshake adds only 100-200ms to every HTTPS request
common_misconceptions.reality: Handshake adds ~300ms per fresh connection. Session resumption reduces this ~50%. Most browsers resume sessions, so repeat connections have minimal overhead. But fresh connections to new domains pay full cost.

common_misconceptions.misconception: SNI makes TLS SNI hostname visible to encryption, breaking privacy
common_misconceptions.reality: SNI hostname is unencrypted (privacy loss is real), but application data is encrypted. Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) in TLS 1.3 RFC 9849 fixes this.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Layer 4 load balancers cannot route TLS traffic intelligently
common_misconceptions.reality: Layer 4 + SNI/ALPN inspection allows intelligent TLS routing without decryption. Layer 7 is only needed if routing depends on HTTP headers or body content.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Certificate pinning is a best practice for security
common_misconceptions.reality: Certificate pinning is outdated and creates more operational risk than security benefit. Industry consensus (OWASP, DigiCert) recommends Certificate Transparency monitoring instead.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Session IDs are better than session tickets because server has direct control
common_misconceptions.reality: Session tickets are superior because they eliminate server-side storage scaling issues. Stateless tickets work better at large scale; session IDs cause cache invalidation headaches.

common_misconceptions.misconception: OCSP checking is the modern way to verify certificate revocation
common_misconceptions.reality: OCSP checking adds 100-300ms latency if not stapled. OCSP stapling (server attaches response) is the modern practice. TLS 1.3 doesn't eliminate need for revocation checking.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Static content with long cache-control max-age breaks updates
common_misconceptions.reality: Cache-busting via URL versioning (hash in filename) + long max-age works perfectly. Client only requests new version when URL changes, avoiding unnecessary revalidation.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Longer certificate chains are more secure
common_misconceptions.reality: Longer chains add latency and validation overhead. Intermediate CAs are necessary for issuer separation, but shorter chains (2-3 intermediaries) are preferable.

common_misconceptions.misconception: 0-RTT in TLS 1.3 is always safe and recommended
common_misconceptions.reality: 0-RTT mode is vulnerable to replay attacks. Recommended for idempotent requests only (GET), not state-changing requests (POST).

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTPS is inherently slower than HTTP
common_misconceptions.reality: Modern HTTPS (TLS 1.3 + session resumption) is nearly as fast as HTTP for repeat connections. Fresh connections pay ~1 RTT overhead. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 compress headers, offsetting small TLS cost.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TCP slow start is independent of TLS handshake
common_misconceptions.reality: TCP slow start begins after handshake completes, so handshake latency directly reduces time before data transfer can be maximized. Fresh connection = slow start + application delay.
